attributes.stem: A 50-year-old woman presents with a painful rash on the right side of her forehead. The rash was preceded by a 2 day history of intermittent burning sensation in the area. She is otherwise fit and healthy without any significant past medical history. She admits that she has been stressed at work recently. On examination, the rash appears localised to the area shown in the photograph (Image).
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
This is a classic example of shingles (herpes zoster) featuring a unilateral vesicopustular eruption which does not cross midline, and is limited to a dermatome. In this case, the patient has herpes zoster ophthalmicus with the right ophthalmic division of the trigeminal nerve being affected. A swab to confirm diagnosis should be taken, and antivirals should be commenced within 72 hours of rash onset. Adequate pain management should be provided to prevent post-herpetic neuralgia, and this patient also warrants an ophthalmology referral for slit lamp examination. Ocular complications such as keratitis and uveitis may occur days to weeks after rash onset and examination should be performed to look for photophobia, change in visual acuity and decrease in corneal sensation.

Shingles is a notifiable disease and patients should be excluded from work or school until the blisters have ruptured and crusty skin changes have resolved. There are two vaccines available in Australia - Zostavax and Shingrix. Zostavax is no longer on the funded immunisation program, has approximately 5 years of protection and is not recommended for people with immunocompromise, but is an option if Shingrix is not available. Shingrix is a 2-dose schedule funded for patients over 65 years of age, adults with severe immunocompromise and Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander people aged >50 years of age.

Herpes simplex is possible but does not present in a unilateral manner along a dermatome.

Psoriasis does not present with papularpustular eruption but rather more scaly itchy patches in appearance.

Contact dermatitis is possible to present in a unilateral manner with pustular eruption, however, it would not follow a dermatome.

Bullous pemphigoid being localised to the face would be highly unusual. There are no other conditions which presents in a unilateral manner along a dermatome, other than herpes zoster.
learningPoints: Dermatomal pain almost always precedes the rash in Herpes zoster infection.
references: eTG complete [Internet]. Melbourne (Vic): Therapeutic Guidelines Ltd; 2019. Shingles [updated 2020 Dec; cited 2021 Feb 11]. Available from: https://tgldcdp.tg.org.au/viewTopic?topicfile=shingles&guidelineName=Antibiotic&topicNavigation=navigateTopic#toc_d1e47
Dermnet NZ [Internet]. 2003 [updated 2016 Jan; cited 2020 Feb 14]. Available from:  https://dermnetnz.org/topics/bullous-pemphigoid/

Australian Technical Advisory Group on Immunisation (ATAGI). Zoster (herpes zoster).Australian Immunisation Handbook, Australian Government Department of Health and Aged Care, Canberra, 2022, immunisationhandbook.health.gov.au.[updated 1 November 2023; cited 23 May 2024]
